item.title: US Copyright Office Will Raise Termination Fee 126%?
item.description: Authors Alliance submitted a comment to the US Copyright Office in response to its proposed fee schedule (Docket No. 2026–2). […]
item.source:markdown: Authors Alliance submitted a comment to the US Copyright Office in response to its proposed fee schedule (Docket No. 2026–2). \[…\]

item.title: Tiger King 2: The Tenth Circuit Sequel on Fair Use in Documentary
item.description: In May 2024, we filed an amicus brief urging the Tenth Circuit to rehear Whyte Monkee Productions v. Netflix. The previous decision was disastrous for documentary filmmakers, historians, and anyone else who uses photographs, movie clips, or other works as historical reference points or for illustration. That’s because the court previously took the position that fair use generally disfavored uses that didn’t directly comment or criticize creative aspects of the underlying work. This meant that all sorts of common uses that engaged with the subject of an underlying work rather than its artistic content were now in jeopardy. It came to this conclusion based on what was, in our view, a significant misreading of the Supreme Court’s recent guidance in Andy Warhol Foundation v. Goldsmith.
item.source:markdown: In May 2024, we filed an amicus brief urging the Tenth Circuit to rehear Whyte Monkee Productions v. Netflix. The previous decision was disastrous for documentary filmmakers, historians, and anyone else who uses photographs, movie clips, or other works as historical reference points or for illustration. That’s because the court previously took the position that fair use generally disfavored uses that didn’t directly comment or criticize creative aspects of the underlying work. This meant that all sorts of common uses that engaged with the subject of an underlying work rather than its artistic content were now in jeopardy. It came to this conclusion based on what was, in our view, a significant misreading of the Supreme Court’s recent guidance in Andy Warhol Foundation v. Goldsmith.

item.title: The Public Interest Corpus: Final Report and Path Forward
item.description:
We are releasing today the final report of the Public Interest Corpus project. A stable, citable version is here: The Public Interest Corpus: A Framework for Implementation. Because we want to encourage additional feedback, we’re also sharing a version here that allows you to comment.
The report is the product of more than a year of work supported by the Mellon Foundation, in which we asked how research libraries can make books data available for AI training and computational research in ways that serve the public interest, rather than reinforcing the existing concentration of access to texts among a small number of well-resourced commercial actors.
item.source:markdown:
We are releasing today the final report of the Public Interest Corpus project. A stable, citable version is here: The Public Interest Corpus: A Framework for Implementation. Because we want to encourage additional feedback, we’re also sharing a version here that allows you to comment.
The report is the product of more than a year of work supported by the Mellon Foundation, in which we asked how research libraries can make books data available for AI training and computational research in ways that serve the public interest, rather than reinforcing the existing concentration of access to texts among a small number of well-resourced commercial actors.

item.title: Learning Comedy
item.description:
John welcomes five-time Emmy-winning writer Ali Barthwell (Last Week Tonight) to ask, can writers learn to be funny? Drawing from her time as a teacher at The Second City, they look at what writers can learn from sketch and improv, and how to reliably find ways to make an audience laugh. We also celebrate the […]

The post Learning Comedy first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
John welcomes five-time Emmy-winning writer Ali Barthwell (Last Week Tonight) to ask, can writers learn to be funny? Drawing from her time as a teacher at The Second City, they look at what writers can learn from sketch and improv, and how to reliably find ways to make an audience laugh. We also celebrate the \[…\]
The post [Learning Comedy](https://johnaugust.com/2026/learning-comedy) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).

item.title: I Made You a Mixtape (Secs. 1001–1010)
item.description: A levy in exchange for an exemption was a novel idea, and it had some merit in theory. In practice, I think the main lesson of the AHRA is that a future levy scheme is probably ill-advised. But regardless, before Congress considers changing the law, it should make sure it understands the law it is changing.
item.source:markdown: A levy in exchange for an exemption was a novel idea, and it had some merit in theory. In practice, I think the main lesson of the AHRA is that a future levy scheme is probably ill-advised. But regardless, before Congress considers changing the law, it should make sure it understands the law it is changing.

item.title: What I learned from a year of Highland Pro
item.description:
One year ago, we launched Highland Pro. I was confident in the app itself, having put it through its paces over its multi-year development journey. I’d written dozens of scripts and novels in Highland over the years, and knew that Highland Pro was the best version we’d ever made. Our beta testers loved it, too. […]

The post What I learned from a year of Highland Pro first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
One year ago, we launched Highland Pro. I was confident in the app itself, having put it through its paces over its multi-year development journey. I’d written dozens of scripts and novels in Highland over the years, and knew that Highland Pro was the best version we’d ever made. Our beta testers loved it, too. \[…\]
The post [What I learned from a year of Highland Pro](https://johnaugust.com/2026/what-i-learned-from-a-year-of-highland-pro) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).

item.title: Something Very Bad is Going to Happen
item.description:
John welcomes writer/showrunner Haley Z. Boston for a post-mortem on her terrifying new series, Something Very Bad is Going to Happen. They look at how the series evolved from initial idea through production, her experience as a first-time showrunner, the special relationship between women and horror, and how she constructs terror on the page. We […]

The post Something Very Bad is Going to Happen first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
John welcomes writer/showrunner Haley Z. Boston for a post-mortem on her terrifying new series, Something Very Bad is Going to Happen. They look at how the series evolved from initial idea through production, her experience as a first-time showrunner, the special relationship between women and horror, and how she constructs terror on the page. We \[…\]
The post [Something Very Bad is Going to Happen](https://johnaugust.com/2026/something-very-bad-is-going-to-happen) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).

item.title: Bartz v. Anthropic Settlement Update: New Date and Time for the Fairness Hearing (you can join online) and Unsealed Objections
item.description: On April 8, Judge Martínez-Olguín issued an order to move the Bartz v. Anthropic settlement fairness hearing to May 14, 2026 at 2:00 p.m. Class members who have timely objected and wish to be heard may join the hearing via the Zoom log-in information found on page 2 of the order. Participants are advised to log-in early to avoid any capacity or connectivity issues.
item.source:markdown: On April 8, Judge Martínez-Olguín issued an order to move the Bartz v. Anthropic settlement fairness hearing to May 14, 2026 at 2:00 p.m. Class members who have timely objected and wish to be heard may join the hearing via the Zoom log-in information found on page 2 of the order. Participants are advised to log-in early to avoid any capacity or connectivity issues.

item.title: Avoidance and Other Anti-Quests
item.description:
John and Craig flip the idea of motivation on its head and ask, what are your characters running from? Using examples across film and TV, they look at the psychological underpinnings of discomfort, and how to use avoidance as a driving force of character and story. We’ve also been away for a minute, so there’s […]

The post Avoidance and Other Anti-Quests first appeared on John August.
item.source:markdown:
John and Craig flip the idea of motivation on its head and ask, what are your characters running from? Using examples across film and TV, they look at the psychological underpinnings of discomfort, and how to use avoidance as a driving force of character and story. We’ve also been away for a minute, so there’s \[…\]
The post [Avoidance and Other Anti-Quests](https://johnaugust.com/2026/avoidance-and-other-anti-quests) first appeared on [John August](https://johnaugust.com).
